question: Are the students' costumes changed from Medieval/Renaissance style (tailored, puffy sleeves) to Ancient Roman style (draped togas or tunics)?
Have the Medieval longswords been replaced with Roman-appropriate weaponry (e.g., gladius short swords or pilum javelins)?
Does the new costume color palette consist of muted, earthy tones (ochre, off-white, deep red) rather than bright velvet or jewel tones?
Is the text on the hand-held placard changed from 'Knights of the Round Table' to a Roman-related phrase (e.g., 'SPQR', 'Senate of Rome')?
Has the whiteboard text mentioning 'Medieval', 'Camelot', or 'Legends' been replaced with Roman Republic-related terms (e.g., 'Republic', 'Consul', 'Tiber')?
Does the modified whiteboard text retain the original marker stroke style, hand-drawn appearance, and line spacing?
Is the whiteboard glare/reflection preserved naturally, overlapping the edited text realistically?
Does the text on the hand-held placard align correctly with the sign's perspective and retain the cardboard material look?
Do the students' hands interact naturally with the new props (weapons, placard) without clipping, floating, or missing fingers?
Are the facial features and identities of all front-row students preserved without alteration?
Are the standing positions and body poses of the front-row students maintained from the original image?
Is the large banner text 'THE ROMAN REPUBLIC' preserved unchanged?
Are background elements (timeline posters, ceiling tiles, whiteboard frame) preserved without distortion?
Are the audience members in the foreground and their recording devices (phone screens) preserved intact?
Are the wooden podium and papers on the desks preserved (excluding replaced weapons)?
Does the lighting on the new costumes match the direction and intensity of the room's ambient light?
Are the edges of the new costumes blended naturally without visible halos or mask artifacts?
Is the image free from severe anatomical distortions, melted textures, or unnatural artifacts?
